title: Multiply with 1 and 0
essential_question: What happens when you multiply a number by 1 or by 0?

pretest.question: What is 7 × 1?
pretest.explanation: Identity Property: any number × 1 = that number. 7 × 1 = 7.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130

pretest.question: Which property states that any number times 1 equals that number?
pretest.explanation: The Identity Property of Multiplication: n × 1 = n.

learn.title: Identity Property of Multiplication
learn.content: Any number multiplied by 1 equals that number. 8 × 1 = 8. 1 × 8 = 8. The number 1 is called the 'identity' because multiplying by 1 leaves the number unchanged — it keeps its identity.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Zero Property of Multiplication
learn.content: Any number multiplied by 0 equals 0. 6 × 0 = 0. 0 × 6 = 0. Think of it this way: 6 groups of 0 objects — there is nothing in any group, so the total is 0!

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Why These Properties Matter
learn.content: These are the easiest multiplication facts! Any fact with 0 as a factor = 0. Any fact with 1 as a factor = the other number. Knowing these two rules instantly solves 19 facts from your times table without any calculation.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Spotting 0 and 1 in Expressions
learn.content: Look at an expression BEFORE you calculate. If you see a 0 anywhere as a factor, the answer is instantly 0. If you see 1 as a factor, the answer is just the other number. Example: 847 × 0 = 0 and 1 × 638 = 638 — no work needed!

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Zero in a Chain of Factors
learn.content: If even ONE factor in a multiplication chain is 0, the whole product is 0. Example: 5 × 9 × 0 × 3 = 0. It does not matter what the other factors are — zero wipes out the product completely.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 1과 0 — 헷갈리지 않는 비교
learn.content: 이 두 규칙은 비슷해 보여서 자주 헷갈립니다. 명확히 구분: n × 1 = n (Identity, 항등원) — 1을 곱하면 그대로. n × 0 = 0 (Zero, 영원) — 0을 곱하면 0. 흔한 실수 1: 5 × 1 = 1로 답함 (1이 다른 수를 '먹어버린다'고 오해). 흔한 실수 2: 5 × 0 = 5로 답함 (덧셈 규칙 '5+0=5'을 곱셈에 잘못 적용). 기억법: '1배 = 그대로, 0배 = 아예 없음'.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 미지수 풀기 — ?×1 = N 패턴
learn.content: Identity Property를 거꾸로 활용하면 미지수를 즉시 찾을 수 있습니다. ? × 1 = 99 → ? = 99 (계산 없이 곱과 같음). ? × 1 = 8 → ? = 8. 유사 패턴: 0 × ? = 0 → ?에는 어떤 수든 가능 (모든 수 정답). 주의: ? × 0 = 5 같은 식은 풀 수 없음 — 0을 곱한 결과는 항상 0이므로 5가 될 수 없음.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 1·0 곱셈 — 즉답 전략 요약
learn.content: 곱셈식을 보고 계산하기 전에 스캔! 1) 0이 하나라도 있으면 → 답 = 0 (다른 인자는 무시). 2) 1이 인자면 → 답 = 다른 인자 (0이 없는 경우). 3) 둘 다 없으면 → 일반 곱셈 진행. 이 전략으로 곱셈표 100개 사실 중 19개(0×n=0의 11개 + 1×n=n의 9개 중복 제외)를 즉답!

feedback.correct: Correct! 4 × 0 = 0. Empty boxes mean zero toys total.
feedback.incorrect: 4 boxes × 0 toys = 4 × 0 = 0. Zero groups of anything = 0.

feedback.incorrect: A, B, C all have 0 as a factor → products are 0. D: 1×1=1 ≠ 0.

try.feedback_correct: Correct! 1 × 1 = 1. It has no zero factor, so it does NOT equal 0.

practice_r1.question: Which is greater: 5 × 1 or 5 × 0?
hints: Calculate each: 5×1=? and 5×0=?
Identity Property: 5×1=5. Zero Property: 5×0=0. Which is larger?

feedback.correct: Right! 5×1=5 and 5×0=0. 5 > 0, so 5×1 is greater.
feedback.incorrect: 5×1=5 (Identity) and 5×0=0 (Zero). 5 is greater than 0.

practice_r1.question: ? × 1 = 8. What is the missing number?
practice_r1.explanation: Identity Property: 8 × 1 = 8. The missing number is 8.
hints: Identity Property: ? × 1 = ? The product equals the missing factor.
Since any number × 1 = that number, the missing number must equal 8.

feedback.correct: Correct! 8 × 1 = 8. The missing factor is 8.
feedback.incorrect: ? × 1 = 8. By the Identity Property, the missing factor equals the product: 8.

practice_r1.question: 0 × ? = 0. Which numbers could fill the blank?
practice_r1.explanation: Zero Property: 0 × any number = 0. Any number works in the blank.
hints: Zero Property: 0 × anything = 0. Try a few numbers.
Does 0×1=0? Yes. Does 0×5=0? Yes. Does 0×100=0? Yes.

feedback.correct: Correct! 0 × any number always equals 0.
feedback.incorrect: Try several numbers: 0×1=0, 0×5=0, 0×99=0. Any number works.

? × 1 = 25. The missing factor must equal the product.

feedback.correct: Correct! 25 × 1 = 25. Identity Property: missing factor = 25.

practice_r1.question: 1 × 0 = ? Enter the number.
practice_r1.explanation: 1 × 0 = 0. (Both Identity and Zero Property apply — Zero Property gives the product of 0.)
hints: One factor is 0. The Zero Property says any number × 0 = 0.
Even though 1 is a factor, 0 is also a factor — and Zero Property always gives 0.

feedback.correct: Correct! 1 × 0 = 0. When 0 is a factor, the product is always 0.
feedback.incorrect: 1 × 0 = 0. The Zero Property: any number × 0 = 0.

practice_r1.feedback_correct: Correct! 1 × 0 = 0. When 0 is a factor, the product is always 0.

practice_r1.question: Sara says: 'Any number times 0 equals that number.' Is she right?
choices: B. No, any number times 0 equals 0
C. No, any number times 0 equals 1
D. It depends on the number
practice_r1.explanation: Sara confused the Zero Property with the Identity Property. Any number × 0 = 0, not that number.
hints: Test Sara's claim with an example: 5 × 0 = ?
Zero Property: any number × 0 = 0 (not the number itself).

feedback.correct: Right! Sara is wrong. Any number × 0 = 0. She confused Zero Property with Identity Property.
feedback.incorrect: Test it: 5 × 0 = 0, not 5. Sara confused the two properties. Answer is B.

practice_r1.feedback_correct: Right! Sara is wrong. Any number × 0 = 0. She confused Zero Property with Identity Property.

practice_r2.question: Caleb has 1 bag with 9 marbles. Sam has 9 bags with 0 marbles each. Who has more marbles?
choices: C. They have the same number
practice_r2.explanation: Caleb: 1×9=9 marbles. Sam: 9×0=0 marbles. Caleb has more.
hints: Calculate each person's total: Caleb = 1 bag × 9 marbles, Sam = 9 bags × 0 marbles.
Identity Property for Caleb: 1×9=9. Zero Property for Sam: 9×0=0.

feedback.correct: Correct! Caleb: 1×9=9. Sam: 9×0=0. Caleb has more marbles.
feedback.incorrect: Caleb: 1×9=9 (Identity). Sam: 9×0=0 (Zero). 9 > 0, so Caleb wins.

practice_r2.feedback_correct: Correct! Caleb: 1×9=9. Sam: 9×0=0. Caleb has more marbles.

Every multiplication by 1 keeps the product as 1.

feedback.correct: Correct! No matter how many times you multiply by 1, the product stays 1.

practice_r2.question: What is 0 × 0?
practice_r2.explanation: Zero Property: 0 × 0 = 0. Zero times any number (even zero) = 0.
hints: 0 is a factor. Zero Property: 0 × anything = 0.
Both factors are 0, but the product is still 0.

feedback.correct: Correct! 0 × 0 = 0. Zero Property applies.
feedback.incorrect: 0 × 0 = 0. Even 0 × 0 follows the Zero Property.

practice_r2.explanation: 9 × 1 = 9 shows the Identity Property: a number × 1 = itself.
hints: Identity Property involves multiplying by 1. Look for 1 as a factor.
Which equation has 1 as a factor and the product equals the other factor?

feedback.correct: Correct! 9 × 1 = 9 shows the Identity Property.
feedback.incorrect: Identity Property: look for 1 as a factor. 9 × 1 = 9 — that's it! Answer C.

practice_r3.question: Emma starts with 7. She multiplies by 1, then by 0, then by 1 again. What is the final answer?
hints: Work step by step: 7 × 1 = ?, then ? × 0 = ?, then ? × 1 = ?
Once 0 appears as a factor, the product becomes 0 — and stays 0.

feedback.correct: Correct! 7×1=7, then 7×0=0, then 0×1=0. Zero kills the product.
feedback.incorrect: Step-by-step: 7×1=7 → 7×0=0 → 0×1=0. Answer is 0.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Correct! 7×1=7, then 7×0=0, then 0×1=0. Zero kills the product.

practice_r3.question: A teacher says: 'The product of any two whole numbers is always greater than each factor.' Is this true?
choices: B. True, except when 0 is a factor
C. True, except when 0 or 1 is a factor
practice_r3.explanation: With 0: product = 0 (less than most factors). With 1: product = the other number (not greater). Both exceptions disprove the claim.
hints: Test with 0: is 5 × 0 = 0 greater than 5? No.
Test with 1: is 4 × 1 = 4 greater than 4? No — it equals 4, not greater.

feedback.correct: Excellent! Both 0 and 1 break the rule. The claim is only true for factors ≥ 2.
feedback.incorrect: 5×0=0 (not greater than 5). 4×1=4 (not greater than 4). Both 0 and 1 are exceptions.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Excellent! Both 0 and 1 break the rule. The claim is only true for factors ≥ 2.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic F — Distributive Property and Properties of Multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r3.question: A train has 8 cars. Each car has 1 engine and 0 swimming pools. How many engines and how many pools on the whole train?
practice_r3.explanation: Engines: 8 cars × 1 engine = 8 × 1 = 8. Pools: 8 cars × 0 pools = 8 × 0 = 0.
hints: Engines: 8 × 1 = ? (Identity Property). Pools: 8 × 0 = ? (Zero Property).
Apply the correct property for each: Identity for engines, Zero for pools.

feedback.correct: Correct! 8×1=8 engines (Identity) and 8×0=0 pools (Zero).
feedback.incorrect: Engines: 8×1=8 (Identity Property). Pools: 8×0=0 (Zero Property).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.7 'Multiply with 1 and 0' pp.127-130

practice_r3.question: Store A sells 0 items each day for 7 days. Store B sells 1 item each day for 7 days. How many total items did BOTH stores sell?
practice_r3.explanation: Store A: 0×7=0. Store B: 1×7=7. Total: 0+7=7 items.
hints: Calculate each store separately: Store A = 0 per day × 7 days, Store B = 1 per day × 7 days.
Store A: Zero Property → 0. Store B: 1×7=7. Add the two results.

feedback.correct: Great! Store A: 0×7=0. Store B: 1×7=7. Together: 0+7=7 items.
feedback.incorrect: Store A: 0×7=0 (Zero Property). Store B: 1×7=7. Total = 0+7 = 7.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Great! Store A: 0×7=0. Store B: 1×7=7. Together: 0+7=7 items.

practice_r3.question: What is 1 × 0 × 1 × 5 × 1? Enter the number.
practice_r3.explanation: 0 is one of the factors. Zero Property: the entire product = 0.
hints: Before calculating, scan the factors. Do you see a 0 anywhere?
Zero in a chain of factors wipes out the entire product. No need to calculate the rest.

feedback.correct: Excellent! You spotted the 0 factor — the entire product is 0. Zero wipes out everything.
feedback.incorrect: 1×0×1×5×1: there is a 0 in the chain. Zero Property: product = 0.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Excellent! You spotted the 0 factor — the entire product is 0. Zero wipes out everything.
